coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots obtained hydrothermally from citric acid and urea: The role of the specific nitrogen centers in their electrochemical and optical responses
coredata.dc:creator: Vercelli, Barbara
Donnini, Riccardo
Ghezzi, Francesco
Sansonetti, Antonio
Giovanella, Umberto
La Ferla, Barbara
coredata.dc:description: The knowledge of how the different types of nitrogen centers affect the electrochemical and optical properties of Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CDs) is of paramount importance in the design and synthesis of these nanostructures. In fact, from the knowledge of the redox centers of N-CDs it is possible tune their energetic levels in order to realize the suitable matches for the design of opto-electronic, photovoltaic or catalytic devices. Herein we present a study of the electrochemical and the optical responses of N-CDs, obtained by hydrothermal method from citric acid and urea. The main issue is to investigate the role played by the specific nitrogen centers in their redox processes. From the voltammetric responses we showed that the observed oxidation processes of N-CDs involve their graphitic and pyrrolic N-atoms, respectively, while the reduction process regards their protonated pyridinic N-atoms. Then, from spectro-electrochemical determinations, we explained that the oxidation of the graphitic N-atoms is a two electron process that gives an N-oxide form through the reaction with a molecule of water and the loss of two protons. Finally we discussed the observed pH-dependence of both the optical and the electrochemical responses of our N-CDs to explain the role played by their pyridinic N-atoms.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen-doped Carbon Quantum Dots
Hydrothermal Method
Electrochemistry
Redox Centers
Optical Properties

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We thanks T. Ranucci and D. Della Torre for the technical support. We are also indebted with the Italian Government for the financial support.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Common Types of N-doping/functionalizations in N-CQDs. Green, blue and gray represent carbon, nitrogen and hydrogen atoms, respectively.

(a) UV-vis spectrum of 0.5 mg ml−1 N-CQDs solution (black line) and PL spectrum of N-CQDs at 345 nm excitation wavelength (red line); (b) UV-vis Spectra of 0.5 mg ml−1 N-CQD solution at progressive dilutions (1:2, 1: 4, 1:16, 1:32). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) UV-vis spectra of 0.5 mg ml−1 N-CQDs solutions in different pH buffers. Inset: linear relationships vs pH of the band intensities. (b) UV-vis spectra of 0.5 mg ml−1 N-CQDs solution: pH reversibility study in the pH range 3-9.

(a) Cyclic voltammogram of 0.5 mg ml−1 N-CDs (black line) and CG electrode (red line) in pH 9 buffer + 0.1 M NaClO4. Scan rate: 0.1 V s−1. Inset: Single sweep voltammogram of N-CQDS CG background subtracted. (b) Cyclic voltammogramms of 1 mg ml−1 of N-CDs in pH 5 buffer + 0.1 M NaClO4. Scan rate: 0.1 V s−1. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Single-sweep cyclic voltammograms of 1 mg ml−1 N-CQDs in pH 5 (black line), 4 (red line) 3 (blue line) and pH 2 (green line) buffers + 0.1 M NaClO4. Scan rate: 0.1 V s−1. Inset: linear relationships of oxidation (red line) and reduction (black line) peak potentials vs pH. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) Cyclic voltammogram of 1 mg ml−1 N-CDs in pH 4 buffer + 0.1 M NaClO4. Scan rate: 0.2, 0.1, 0.05, 0.02 V s−1. (b) Differential Pulsed voltammogramms of 1 mg ml−1 of N-CDs in pH 4 buffer + 0.1 M NaClO4.

(a) UV-vis spectra of 0.5 mg ml−1 N-CQDs solution at increasing charge. Applied potential 0.5 V vs SCE. Electrolytic medium 0.1 M NaClO4/pH 9 buffer. (b) UV-vis spectrum of N-CQDs (black line) and of NO-CQDs (red line). Inset: .(1): single sweep cyclic voltammograms of N-CQDs (black line) and NO-CQDs (red line); (2): FTIR spectrum of NO-CQDs. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Dependence vs pH of the un-protonated pyridine N-atom: experimental (red-line) and theoretical (blue-line) curves. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
XPS binding energy values (eV) of N-CDs.
Oxidation (Eox /V) and reduction (Ered/V) peak potentials vs SCE and peak width (ΔE/mV) for N-CDs at different pH.

head.ce:title: Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots obtained hydrothermally from citric acid and urea: The role of the specific nitrogen centers in their electrochemical and optical responses

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: eDipartimento di Biotecnologie e Bioscienze, Università degli Studi di Milano-Bicocca, Piazza della Scienza, 2–20126, Milano, Italy

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The knowledge of how the different types of nitrogen centers affect the electrochemical and optical properties of Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CDs) is of paramount importance in the design and synthesis of these nanostructures. In fact, from the knowledge of the redox centers of N-CDs it is possible tune their energetic levels in order to realize the suitable matches for the design of opto-electronic, photovoltaic or catalytic devices. Herein we present a study of the electrochemical and the optical responses of N-CDs, obtained by hydrothermal method from citric acid and urea. The main issue is to investigate the role played by the specific nitrogen centers in their redox processes. From the voltammetric responses we showed that the observed oxidation processes of N-CDs involve their graphitic and pyrrolic N-atoms, respectively, while the reduction process regards their protonated pyridinic N-atoms. Then, from spectro-electrochemical determinations, we explained that the oxidation of the graphitic N-atoms is a two electron process that gives an N-oxide form through the reaction with a molecule of water and the loss of two protons. Finally we discussed the observed pH-dependence of both the optical and the electrochemical responses of our N-CDs to explain the role played by their pyridinic N-atoms.

Study of the role played by the specific nitrogen centers in the oxidation/reduction processes of Nitrogen-doped Carbon Quantum Dots.
Image, graphical abstract

ce:section.ce:para: Interest in carbon quantum dots (CDs), a type of fluorescent carbon nanoparticles, has been steadily increasing since their discovery by Xu et al.[1]. Their size, crystallinity and composition-dependent properties make CDs very promising materials for application in the field of energy conversion and storage [2,3], catalysis [4], synthesis [5–8], sensing[9–12], photovoltaic[13], lighting[14] or biological applications [15,16]. It is expected that they can replace the well-known quantum dots based on metal calcogenides as a result of their excellent biocompatibility and solubility, low toxicity, high photostability against bleaching and blinking and their preparation methods that are low cost and green [17,18]. Fluorescence quantum yield (QY) is an important metric that quantifies the ability of a particle to release absorbed electromagnetic radiation as photons. A nitrogen doping agent is commonly added in the CDs synthesis process since it has been well documented that N-doped CDs (N-CDs) have enhanced QY when compared to non-doped CDs [19,20]. It is reported that in N-CDs, some of the carbon atoms at the core/edges of the CDs honeycomb matrix are replaced by nitrogen atoms, or N-containing groups reside at edges. The most common types of N-atoms found in N-CDs are graphitic, pyridinic, pyrrolic, and amino centers (Scheme 1 ) [19]. Experimental studies have shown that the absorption and emission bands of N-CDs, shift to longer wavelengths as the nitrogen content increases [18,21–23], however they do not report how the optical properties of N-CDs are affected by introducing specific types of nitrogen centers. Only a theoretical work on the absorption spectra of N-CDs tries to shed some light on these complex processes [19]. In the same way, the electrochemical properties of N-CDs are less investigated and to the best of our knowledge there are no electrochemical studies dealing with the role played by the specific nitrogen centers in the oxidation/reduction processes of N-CDs. Most studies investigate the electro-catalytic activity of CDs coated electrodes toward different model redox probes and electroactive analytes with a view of potential use in electro-analysis [24,25]. Some works study the intrinsic electrochemistry of CDs in order to characterize this type of compounds [26,27]. Two recent works report, respectively, that the fluorescence of N-CDs is reversibly switchable “on-off” via redox reactions [28] and that the CDs luminescence depends on their redox states [29]. Cliffel et al. [30] performed a systematic study of the optical and electrochemical responses of a series of N-CDs compared with non-doped CDs; they suggest a possible role played by the pyridinic and pyrrolic N-atoms. Starting from these considerations in the present work we studied the electrochemical and optical responses of N-CDs obtained hydrothermally from citric acid and urea; the main issue is to identify their oxidation and reduction centers. N-CDs were synthetized following a previously reported hydrothermal route[ 31] which employs citric acid and urea, as bio-synthesizable and low cost precursors. In fact, citric acid is a common source of carbon in bottom-up CDs synthesis and it can be also synthetized by the fungus Aspergillus Niger, allowing for its production through bio-processes [32]. Urea acted both as base and as N-doping source and it is a readily available and cheap reactant. The obtained samples were characterized by means of UV-vis, FT-IR and Fluorescence (PL) emission spectroscopies, X-ray Diffraction (XRD), X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) and Cyclic Voltammetry. From the voltammetric responses we determined the oxidation and reduction centers of our samples. Then we described the mechanism of the first oxidation process of our N-CDs through a spectro-electrochemical study supported by FT-IR determinations. Finally we discussed the observed pH-dependence of the optical and electrochemical responses of our samples to explain the role played by the pyridine N-atoms.

All used chemicals and solvents were reagent grade and used as received. pH buffers (2, 3, 4, 5, 7, 9, 12) for UV-vis and electrochemical analyses were purchased by Carlo Erba. Nitrogen-doped CDs (N-CDs) were prepared according to a reported route [31]. 2 g (1 mmol) of citric acid and 1.9 g (3 mmol) of urea were dissolved in 50 mL of deionized water, transferred into a Teflon-lined stainless-steel reactor (100 mL) and heated at 160°C for 4h. After the reactor was cooled to room temperature naturally, the solution was filtrated through a microporous membrane (0.22 μm) to remove the large particles and precipitated with EtOH followed by centrifugation. The re-dispersion/precipitation procedure was repeated three times to ensure the effective removing of precursor residues and small derivatives. The final product was obtained as blue solid by drying the samples at 60°C for 1 h. The solids were stored in closed vials at 4°C. The synthesis route was repeated three times. The good reproducibility of the properties of the obtained materials proved its reliability. Furthermore, the ninhydrin-test performed on our N-CDs samples excludes the presence of primary amine-containing residues, see Fig. A1. The reaction yield was ca 45 % in all cases.
UV-vis spectra were collected with a Perkin Elmer Lambda 35 spectrometer; FTIR spectra of KBr pellets were recorded on a Nicolet 6700 spectrometer, 64 scans, resolution 4 cm−1.

The continuous wave PL spectra were recorded by liquid nitrogen cooled charge-coupled device combined with a monochromator, and excited using a monochromated xenon lamp. PL-QYs in solutions were measured by comparison with quinine in 1N H2SO4 water solution as a standard.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectra were recorded using a Siemens D500 X-ray diffractometer with Cu-Kα radiation (λ = 0.15418 nm), and collected in the 2Θ range of 10°–40°, with a step size of 0.01° and 5 sec/step.

The XPS apparatus consisted in a VSW CLASS 100 hemispherical analyzer, equipped with a single channel detector, with a non–monochromated Al/Mg XRay source (VSW model TA10). The apparatus is equipped with an impact ionization ion gun VG EX05 with a raster scan unit (SAX 346) for imaging and depth profiling. Ar, Ne and He gas can be used. The core level fits have been performed through a Voigt profile including a Lorentzian function (accounting for lifetime broadening) and a Gaussian function (accounting for the finite instrumental resolution). The fitting routine included also a Shirley background, mimicking the secondary electron background [33]. C1s line shape was instead fitted with a Doniach-Sunjic's curve using an asymmetry factor typically of 0.05. Atomic sensitivity factors were specifically determined for the apparatus using the Lindau's cross sections [33].

Electrochemistry was performed at room temperature in three electrode cells; counter electrode was platinum; reference electrode was SCE. Working electrodes were glassy carbon (GC 0.06 cm2). Supporting electrolyte was 0.1 M NaClO4. Cyclic voltammetry (CV) was performed at a scan rate of 0.1 V s−1. The voltammetric apparatus was Metrohm Autolab 128N potentiostat/galvanostat. CVs were performed in 1 mg ml−1 N-CD solutions. Differential Pulsed Voltammetry (DPV) response was maximized at pulse amplitude A = 50 mV, pulse duration T = 50 ms and scan rate v = 0.02 V s−1.

Optical properties – The absorption spectrum of our N-CDs solutions (Fig 1a) shows three absorption bands: at 240 nm, 340 nm and 630 nm, respectively. The peak at 240 nm is ascribed to the π–π* transition of aromatic sp2 domains [34,35] and usually results in no observed photoluminescence (PL) emission[20]. The other strong absorption peak at 340 nm is assigned to the n–π* transition of possible C=O or C=N groups present in N-CDs structure [20,28,36]. The peak at 630 nm was reported only by Wang [28] and it is not ascribed to the formation of carbon dots aggregates in solution. In fact, as Fig. 1b shows, upon progressive dilution of N-CDs solutions up to 1:32, the intensities of the peaks at 340 nm and at 630 nm decrease linearly with dilution (see Fig, 1b inset) keeping constant their ratio, thus showing that there is no dynamic conversion from the peak at 630 nm to the one at 340 nm or vice versa. Similar to Wang's case [28], the peak at 630 nm shows that our N-CDs are in an oxidized state. In fact, after the reducing agent hydrazine was added into N-CDs solutions the blue solution turned to colorless and the peak at 630 nm disappeared (Fig. A2). We also observed a dependence vs pH of the UV-vis responses of our N-CDs. As Fig. 2a shows, both the intensity and the wavelength of the band assigned to the n-π* transition vary in the low pH region and remain almost unchanged in the higher pH one. It is interesting to note the linear decrease of the peak intensity in the pH range 7-2 (Fig. 2a, inset). Similarly the color of the solutions endures a rapid transformation to darker in the acidic pH region (Fig. A3), and remains stable in the basic pH one. The spectra reported in Fig. 2a were collected in buffered solution. So, to check the reproducibility/reversibility of the observed pH-dependence, we performed the UV-vis analyses by changing the pH from 3 to 9 and vice versa, with the alternate addition of concentrated NaOH and HClO4 into the same N-CDs solution to adjust the pH at the desired values. As Fig. 2b shows, the intensity of the band first increases significantly (pH from 3 to 9) and then it restores nearly as before (pH from 9 to 3). Even after four cycles of pH variation the intensity of the band remains virtually unchanged (Fig. 2b inset). Conversely, the wavelength shifts observed in Fig. 2a were not observed in Fig. 2b. Thus we suspect that those shifts could be a possible effect of the specific buffer rather than of the pH. The pH dependence of the N-CDs optical properties, in particular PL emission, was already reported in the literature[20,37,38]. In general, the phenomenon was ascribed to the protonation/deprotonation of the N-atoms present in the honey comb matrix of N-CDs that act as fluorophores (Scheme 1). Here, we suggest that the protonation/deprotonation of the pyridinic N-atoms (Scheme 1) could be responsible of the observed pH-dependence, as will be further explained in the following discussion.

N-CDs PL-emission band is peaked at 436 nm (bright blue emission, Fig. A4), upon 345 nm excitation (Fig. 1a) with a Stokes shift of ca 100 nm. The full width at half maximum is ca 80 nm. The PL quantum yield (QY) of 0.37 (measured by using quinine sulfate as reference) was ca 40%, comparable previous reports [28] and decreases if N-CDs are in oxidized state.

N-CDs Characterization – FTIR analyses revealed that the functional groups bounded to the edges of the basal planes of our N-CDs crystal structure are mainly amides and ammonium carboxylates. In fact, as Fig. A5 shows, the amide/ammonium N-H stretching at 3400 cm−1 and 3160 cm−1, the amide C=O stretching at 1680 cm−1, the carboxylate COO− asymmetric stretching at 1570 cm−1, the amide N-H bending at 1472 cm−1, the carboxylate COO− symmetric stretching at 1400 cm−1 and the ammonium N-H bending at 1389 cm−1 bands are present.

XRD diffractogram, Fig. A6, shows a broad peak between 20° and 35°, along with sharp peaks at 26.8° and 29.0°. Thus, it seems that our N-CDs samples contain both crystalline and amorphous phases of carbon [39].

The chemical composition of N-CDs was measured by XPS spectroscopy (Table 1 and Fig.A7). In wide-scan spectra three peaks centered at 285.6 eV, 400.6 eV and 531.6 eV were assigned to C1s, N1s, O1s, respectively. The high resolution C1s spectrum (Fig. A7) was deconvoluted into six peaks including a C=C peak at a binding energy of ca 286.3 eV, a C-N/C=N peak with a binding energy at ca 285.2 eV, a C-O peak with a binding energy at ca 286.1 eV, a C=O peak at a binding energy of ca 287.3 eV, a O-C=O peak at ca 288.5 eV [19,28,40] and a shake-up peak with a binding energy at ca 289.eV [41]. The shake-up peak is a characteristic of conjugated systems or aromatic groups, which indicates the presence of hexagonal graphene-like structure inside the CDs [41]. The high resolution N1s spectrum (Fig.A7) revealed four types of N-atoms, pyridinic type at 399.6 eV, pyrrolyc type at 400.6 eV, graphitic-type N at 401.7 eV [19,28] and pyridine-oxide type at 402.7 eV [42]. The main peak of O1s (Fig. A7) was deconvoluted into four peaks related to the adsorbed water at 532.4 eV, the C=O at 531.4 eV and the C-OH at 533.9 eV. The peak intensity ratio N1s/C1s (RN/C) was calculated from widescan XPS spectra. The RN/C is around 0.14 in somewhat agreement with literature reported data [19,28]. The calculated atomic percentages in the N1s fitting data for pyridinic at 399.6 eV is 12%, for pyrrolic at 400.6 eV is 36 %, for graphitic at 401.7 eV is 34 % and for pyridine-oxide is 18 %.

Electrochemistry – In pH 9 buffer, N-CDs samples exhibited two irreversible oxidation processes at 0.55 V and 1.44 V vs SCE (Fig. 3a). The peak at 0.55 V showed to be dependent on the square root of the scan rate indicating a process controlled by diffusion. We supposed that the N-atoms present in the honey comb matrix of N-CDs [12] may act as oxidation centers. Starting from a theoretical study on the effects of the different N-atoms types on the absorption properties of N-CDs [19], we estimated the following oxidation potentials sequence: graphitic < pyrrolic ≤ amine-like < pyridinic, i.e. the graphitic N-atoms are easier to oxidize while pyridinic ones are harder, see SI for more details. Thus we may suggest that the oxidation process at 0.55 V is attributed to the graphitic N-atoms oxidation, while the one at 1.44 V is assigned to the pyrrolic N-atoms oxidation, see SI. The pyridinic N-atoms are not interested by the oxidation processes because their estimated oxidation potential is too high (see SI) and falls outside the electrochemical window of the GC working electrode in pH 9 buffer [43]. We exclude that the observed voltammetric signals are due to amine-like N-atoms, because in solutions at pH ≤ 7 the amine are protonated and not oxidizable [44]. Conversely, the voltammetric response at 0.55 V depends vs pH (Fig. 4) and the one at 1.44V is also present in N-CDs solutions at pH 7. Furthermore, the ninhydrin-test performed on our N-CDs samples excludes the presence of primary amine residues. It is interesting to note that the potential of the first oxidation peak shifts to higher values as the pH is decreased, moving from 0.55 V at pH 7 to 0.69 V at pH 2 (Table 2 ). The increase is essentially linear in the pH range 7-2 (Fig. 4 inset). The peak width is 75 mV at pH 7 but increases to ca 120 mV at pH 2 (Table 2). We did not ascribe the phenomenon to two overlapped oxidation processes, as both the voltammetric responses at varying scan rate (Fig. 5a) and the differential pulsed voltammetry analyses (Fig. 5b) of our N-CDs performed in pH 3 and pH 4 buffers, clearly show a single oxidation process. Thus it appears that the electron transfer rate of our N-CDs samples decreases in acidic media. In the pH range 7-12 the peak potential remains almost unchanged and the peak width is 65 mV at all pH values indicating a fast electron transfer rate. We also observed that at pH ≤ 5 our N-CDs show an irreversible reduction process at ca -1.22 V (Figs. 4, 3b and Table 2). The reduction potential increases linearly with pH, moving from -1.22 V at pH 5 to -0.94 V at pH 2 (Fig. 4, inset). The electron transfer rate is slow in all cases (Table 2). In the pH range 7-12 no reduction processes were observed. An electrochemical study on pyridine [45], reports the appearance of a reduction peak at ca -1.1 V at pH ≤ 6.5, that was assigned to the mono-electronic reduction of its protonated form, pyridinium ion. So, we may suggest that the observed reduction process in the pH ≤ 5 region is assigned to the reduction of the N-CDs protonated pyridinic N-atoms, as will be further explained in the discussion in the following.

We, also, performed the electrolysis of our N-CDs at 0.5 V in pH 9 buffered solution and we monitored the process by UV-vis spectroscopy. The absorption analysis revealed the progressive decrease of the band at 340 nm and the parallel emergence of a new absorption band at ca 400 nm (Fig. 6a and b). At the same time, the color of the solution turns from blue to dark-green and finally to gold-yellow. The total charge involved in the oxidation process is 8.1 C. The two isosbestic points at ca 300 nm and ca 370 nm indicate the conversion of the graphitic N-atoms into a new form, called NO-CDs. The electrochemical response of NO-CDs shows an irreversible oxidation process at ca 1.6 V vs SCE (Fig. 6b, inset 1), which is the already observed oxidation of the pyrrolic N-atoms that were not involved in the electrolysis process. This means that the graphitic N-atoms were all converted into full oxidized N-atoms. According to Scheme 2 , we may suggest a two electron oxidation process of the graphitic N-atoms to give an N-oxide form through reaction with a molecule of water and the loss of two protons. To confirm this hypothesis we performed the FT-IR analyses of NO-CDs. In fact, the aromatic N-oxides show a strong N-O stretching band at 1255-1300 cm−1 and an out-of-plane bending band at 970-950 cm−1 [46]. The FT-IR spectrum of our NO-CDs (Fig. 6b inset 2) shows a band at 1086 cm−1 broad and multiple assigned to the borate ion H2BO3 − [47], present in the pH 9 buffer, and a band at 776 cm−1 due to the out-of-plane C-H bending at N-CDs edges, as for coronene (769 cm−1). Thus the whole spectrum of NO-CDs indicates that the N-oxides are protonated forms, N-OH+ with an outer shell of N-CDs as carboxylate and amide substituents. Furthermore, a spectroscopic study on pyridine (Py), pyridine-N-oxide (Py-O) and its cation (Py-O+) reports that going from Py-O to Py-O+ molecules the calculated intensity of the N-O stretching band is decreased by a factor of 23 for the Py-O+ molecule with respect to the Py-O one [46]. Thus the stretching band of the protonated N-oxides is difficult to observe in NO-CDs FT-IR spectrum. However a further band at 3300 cm−1 (starred in Fig. 6b inset 2) assigned to the O-H stretching of the protonated N-oxide form, N-OH+, is present [48] in the FT-IR spectrum. Thus, from these considerations the oxidation reaction of N-CDs graphitic N-atoms could be re-written as follows:
N − C D s +
H 2
O − 2
e −
→ N O
H +
− C D s +
H +
i.e. it is a two electron oxidation process to give a protonated N-oxide form, N-OH+ through reaction with a molecule of water and the loss of one proton.

The atomic percentages of the N-atoms types in N-CDs could be estimated from their peak current values. In particular, we have to consider that the reduction of the protonated pyridinic N-atoms is a slow irreversible monoelectronic process whose peak current is 78% of a reversible process. In addition, as above described the oxidation of the graphitic N-atoms, is a fast two electrons process and the same considerations could be done for the pyrrolic N-atoms. So, the estimated atomic percentages of the N-atoms types are ca 26% for pyridinic, ca 40% for graphitic and ca 33% for pyrrolic. The values are in somewhat agreement with the calculated atomic percentages in the N1s fitting data of XPS determinations.

From the above reported results some considerations could be done on the pH-dependence of the optical and electrochemical responses of our N-CDs samples. Concerning the UV-vis analyses, we ascribed the pH-dependence of the N-CDs absorption responses to the protonation/deprotonation of the pyridinic N-atoms present in their honeycomb matrix. However, if we define A the absorbance of the un-protonated pyridinic N-atoms (PyN) in the pH region below 5 and we consider constant,
A
m a x
, their absorbance in the pH region between 7 and 12, the relationship between the fraction of un-protonated pyridinic N-atoms (
A
A
m a x
) vs pH (Fig. 7 ) does not follow the theoretical sigmoidal curve but in the pH region below 5 presents a linear response plot, with intercept of 0.16 and slope of 0.15. atom: experimental (red-line) and theoretical (blue-line) curves.

This behavior may suggest that the acid dissociation of the protonated pyridinic N-atoms may increase with increasing the degree of protonation. Roach [49] observed and described a similar phenomenon in poly-pyridine systems. So, if we consider the acid dissociation of protonated pyridine, its dissociation constant (
p
K a
) is related to
p H
as follows:
(1)
p H =
p
K a
+ log
(
1 − α
α
)
where α is the fraction of protonated pyridine.

In the case of poly-pyridine systems, Roach proposes that Eq. (1) becomes:[49]
(2)
p H =
p
K a
+ q ×
log
(
1 − α
α
)
where q is generally greater than one and takes into account the variations of the electrostatic interactions arising from the progressive protonation of pyridine moieties along the polymeric chains. In fact, protonated pyridine residues repulse hydronium ions, thus hindering their acquisition by neighboring residues and increasing the observed acid dissociation constant. Moreover, in Roach work [48],
p
K a
shows a linear decrease with increasing α in the α range between 0.4 and 0.8. In this range we may write
(3)
p H = p
K 0
− k α
where
p
K 0
is the instrinsic dissociation constant, whose values are linearly extrapolated as the value of
p H
at α = 0.

If we define C the concentration of the total pyridine N-atoms (protonated and un-protonated) present in our N-CDs samples, we may write:
A
A
m a x
=
[
P y N
]
C
= 1 − α

In the
p H
region below 5, the response of
A
A
m a x
vs
p H
(Fig. 5) is linear according to
A
A
m a x
= 0.16 + 0.15 pH
so we may write:
(4)
1 − α = 0.16 + 0.15 pH
which could be re-arranged
(5)
pH = 5.60 − 6.67 α

Hence from Eqs. (3) and (5) we obtain:
p
K 0
= 5.6
for
α = 0
p
K a
= 2.2
for
α = 0.5
In addition, according to Eq. (2) from the plot of
p H
vs
log (
A
A
m a x
− A
)
in the
p H
region below 5 we estimate
q = 3.4
and
p
K a
= 2.2
.

It is interesting to note that the estimated
p
K 0
value of 5.6, estimated for the pyridinic N-atoms of our N-CDs samples, is more similar to the
p
K a
of a protonated acridine (5.68[50]) than to the
p
K a
of a protonated pyridine (5.2[43]). Thus we may argue that the pyridinic N-atoms are like conjugated acridine systems at the edges of the honey comb matrix of our N-CDs.

The
p
K a
= 2.2
in the low pH region confirms our hypothesis on the increase of the acid dissociation of the protonated pyridinic N-atoms by increasing the degree of protonation and explains the observed deviation from the theoretical sigmoidal curve (Fig. 7). The
q = 3.4
indicates that repulsive electrostatic interactions arise upon progressive protonation. So, the observed increased acidity of the pyridinic N-atoms could be explained as a combination of the inductive effect coming from their conjugation and the electrostatic repulsive effect coming from their progressive protonation.

Moreover, from Eq. (4) we can write:
(6)
[
P y N
]
C
= 0.16 − 0.15 log
[
H +
]
which is a polynomial curve (Fig. A8a). By reporting
C
[
P y N
]
vs
[
H +
]
0.5
we observed a linear dependence (Fig. A8b) according to:
(7)
C
[
P y N
]
= 1.8 + 4.5
[
H +
]
0.5

In low pH region we may write
C
[
P y N
]
= 4.5
[
H +
]
0.5
, which could be re-arranged as:
(8)
[
H +
]
[
P y N
]
2
= c o n s t a n t
Eq. (8) clearly shows the pH dependence of UV-vis responses of our N-CDs in the acidic region is influenced by the protonation of their pyridine N-atoms..

Moreover, as above reported concerning the pH-dependence of N-CDs voltammetric responses, we observed that in the low pH region the dependence of oxidation peak potential (
E p
) of N-CDs vs pH is linear (Fig. 3, inset) with a slope of ca -50 mV pH−1 according to:
(9)
E p
=
0.795 − 0.053 pH = a + R T / F ln
[
H
+
]

The relation between peak potential and the transfer rate constant is:
(10)
E p
= c − R T / F ln
k 0 2
(see SI for more information)

So, from Eqs. (9) and (10) we have:
c − R T / F ln
k 0 2
=
a + R T / F ln
[
H
+
]
where:
(11)
[
H
+
]
k 0 2
= c o n s t a n t

From Eqs. (8) and (11) we have:
(12)
k 0
∝
[
P y N
]
which directly relates the electron transfer rate and concentration of the un-protonated pyridine N-atoms.

Eq. (12) clearly shows that in the low pH region the oxidation of the graphitic N-atoms of our N-CDs samples depends on the protonation of their pyridinic N-atoms. In particular, upon the progressive protonation of the pyridinic N-atoms the electron transfer rate of the oxidation of the graphitic N-atoms decreases, explaining the trend reported in Table 2. In addition, it is confirmed the above reported assignment of the reduction process observed at pH ≤ 5 (Fig. 4 and Table 2) to the mono-electronic reduction of the protonated pyridinic N-atoms [45]. Furthermore, regarding the dependence of potential vs pH, this result may also explain how for the reduction process the potential decreases at much significant rate compared to the oxidation one.

The voltammetric and UV-vis responses of N-CDs, obtained hydrothermally from citric acid and urea were analyzed and discussed. From the analysis of the voltammetric responses we showed that the electrochemical oxidation processes of our N-CDs involve their graphitic and pyrrolic N-atoms, respectively while the reduction process observed in the low pH region regards the protonated pyridinic N-atoms. In particular, the oxidation of the graphic N-atoms revealed to be a two electron process that gives an N-oxide form through reaction with a molecule of water and the loss of two protons. Furthermore, from the studies on the pH-dependence of both the optical and the electrochemical responses of N-CDs, we showed that, in the former case, the intensity of the band assigned to the n-π* transition is influenced by the protonation/deprotonation of the pyridinic N-atoms, i.e. it decreases with protonation and viceversa. Moreover, in the acidic pH region the acidity of the protonated pyridinic N-atoms increases by increasing their degree of protonation. The phenomenon was explained by a combination of both the inductive effect coming from the conjugation of the pyridinic N-atoms in our N-CDs honey-comb matrix and the electrostatic repulsive effect coming from their progressive protonation. Regarding the electrochemical responses, we showed that in the acidic pH region the electron transfer rate of the oxidation the graphitic N-atoms of N-CDs is influenced by protonation of the pyridinic N-atoms, i.e. it decreases progressively with protonation. Further studies are still in course to investigate the influence of the reaction conditions on the electrochemical and optical properties of N-CDs, as we believe that these results could be useful in the rational design and synthesis of these nanostructures. In fact, recent works show that CDs can be promising components in organic photovoltaic devices (OPVs) [51] and in light emitting diodes (OLEDs) [52]. Thus, the detailed knowledge of their oxidation and reduction processes enables the fine tuning their energetic levels in order to realize the suitable level alignments for the design of opto-electronic or photovoltaic. Furthermore, according to the recent developments of field effect transistors and super-capacitors [53–55], it is not excluded a possible application of CDs, also, in these systems.

Barbara Vercelli: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing – original draft. Riccardo Donnini: Formal analysis. Francesco Ghezzi: Formal analysis. Antonio Sansonetti: Formal analysis. Umberto Giovanella: Formal analysis. Barbara La Ferla: Writing – review & editing, Formal analysis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We thanks T. Ranucci and D. Della Torre for the technical support. We are also indebted with the Italian Government for the financial support.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.electacta.2021.138557.
UV-vis and FT-IR spectra, XRD pattern, Cyclic Voltammograms, Graphic Rapresentations, Estimated oxidations potentials, Peak potential vs rate constant relation.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon dots as a sustainable new platform for organic light emitting diode
